context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m stuck on the city case study data table rn 😅 can u help me figure out what info I need for my city first?
exchanges.tutor: Hey! Happy to help you work through it. Before we dig in — have you had a chance to read through the assignment description? It actually lists the required data categories pretty clearly. What city did you choose, and what have you already identified as things you need to gather?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a basic orientation question about the assignment. I should point them back to the assignment itself using guided discovery rather than listing everything for them. A simple question can help them engage with the assignment description directly.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but I’m still fuzzy tbh. I picked Mexico City, and I think I need population, area, income, GDP, race/ethnicity maybe, and climate zone?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid start — you've got most of them! Take another quick look at the assignment description. Is there anything on your list you might be missing? Once you've confirmed the full list, which category are you planning to tackle first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified most of the required categories correctly. I should acknowledge this without giving them the complete list, and nudge them to check if they missed anything by going back to the assignment description. I also want to get them moving toward actually finding the data.

exchanges.student: oh wait, location too lol. I guess I’d start w/ location + population since those seem easiest.
exchanges.tutor: Good catch! Sounds like you've got a complete list now. Go for it — what have you found so far for location and population?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified all the required categories. I should confirm they have a complete list without spelling it out for them, and encourage them to start gathering data. I want to move them toward actually doing the work.

exchanges.student: for location I’d say central Mexico, in the Valley of Mexico? population idk if I should use city proper or metro, that part’s messing me up.
exchanges.tutor: Good question — and it's actually an important one for urban studies. What do you think the difference between those two figures would tell you? Which one might be more useful for studying climate vulnerability and urban conditions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable start on location but it could be more precise. More importantly, they're raising a genuinely good methodological question about city proper vs. metro population. Rather than answering it, I should turn it back to them - this is a meaningful analytical choice they should think through in the context of an urban studies course.

exchanges.student: maybe metro is better bc the actual urban area spills way past the city limits? but now I’m not sure if the table wants the official city number fr.
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning is sound. Here's a thought — does it have to be one or the other? What could you do in your table to be transparent about which figure you're using and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good analytical reasoning about why metro might be more relevant. Rather than resolving their uncertainty for them, I should affirm their reasoning and push them to make a decision and note it, which is good research practice. The assignment doesn't specify, so this is a judgment call the student should own.

exchanges.student: ohh maybe I can use the official city population in the main table and note the metro pop in a comment/source note. does that make sense?
exchanges.tutor: That makes a lot of sense — noting your sources and methodological choices is good research practice. Now, what have you found for the other categories like GDP, income per capita, or the Köppen climate zone?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a practical and methodologically sound solution on their own. I should affirm this briefly and push them to move forward to the next data categories rather than dwelling on this point.

exchanges.student: I haven’t looked those up yet ngl. I’m also confused how to find race/ethnicity for Mexico City bc it doesn’t seem listed the same way as in US cities.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really perceptive observation. Why do you think racial/ethnic categories might be measured or reported differently in Mexico than in the US? And where do you think you might look to find how Mexico officially categorizes its population?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a genuinely interesting and valid cross-cultural data challenge. Rather than explaining the difference for them, I should guide them to think about why the categories might differ and where they might look. This is a good learning moment about data comparability across contexts.

exchanges.student: maybe bc census categories are country-specific, so they define identity diff? I’d prob check Mexico’s census agency first, but idk what terms they use.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Mexico's national census agency is a good starting point — see what you find there. What terms or categories come up when you search?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well about why census categories differ cross-culturally. I should affirm this and nudge them toward actually searching for Mexico's census agency rather than dwelling on the concept. They're ready to do the work.

exchanges.student: I’m seeing INEGI and some stuff on indigenous identity, but not like a full race breakdown. is it okay if I use the official categories they report even if it’s not a neat US-style list?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, using the official categories as reported is exactly the right approach — that's intellectually honest research. Make sure to note your source. You've made good progress on location, population, and race/ethnicity. You still have GDP, income per capita, and Köppen climate zone to go — how are you planning to tackle those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has found the right source and is making a sound methodological judgment. This is a good moment to affirm their thinking briefly and remind them of the bigger picture - they still have several data categories left to tackle and we're getting close to the planned conversation length.

exchanges.student: ok yeah that clicked. I’m thinking World Bank/OECD for income or GDP, and maybe a climate map source for Köppen, but I’m not 100% sure which is best.
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid instincts — go try them and see what you find. At this point you have a pretty clear picture of what you need. Have you started filling anything into the actual table yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good instincts about sources. I should briefly affirm and push them to actually go look rather than discussing sources abstractly. We're near the end of the planned conversation length so I want to encourage them to synthesize and start filling in their table.
